content:
Six uncomfortable questions to peel back your titles, lower the apple, and write your true story.

9 min read
Just now

--

Look at René Magritte’s famous painting, The Son of Man. You see a man in a crisp suit, a formal bowler hat, standing perfectly straight. But his face is entirely hidden by a floating green apple.
That painting is the perfect metaphor for how we live today.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image source: https://www.renemagritte.org/the-son-of-man.jsp
In 2026, we aren’t exactly suffering from a shortage of self-introduction templates. You know the drill. Where you graduated, your latest corporate title, the big projects, the awards. You’ve probably even run your resume through an AI prompt to “extract key selling points” and polish the edges. Done. You have a clean, flawless professional bio.
But that bio is just the green apple. It’s an external identity floating in front of your face, hiding who you actually are from the world — and worse, from yourself.
There’s nothing wrong with a polished profile. But let’s be honest: does it actually capture how you live?
Our real lives don’t live in summaries. They live in the messy, quiet space of our choices — the tiny daily trade-offs and the massive leaps. Those choices are shaped by who we are when no one is watching, and whether our environment pushes us toward adventure or safety.
Lately, we’ve been spending so much energy curating the apple that we’ve almost forgotten the person standing behind it. Social media has made it worse. Over the past decade, we stopped sharing our genuine stories and started living for the content that grabs the most likes. Less organic living, more planned broadcasting.
Yet, your true self is still in there. Your core values are like diamonds, but they are hidden behind a thick fog. That fog is made of everyone else’s expectations — what your family wanted for you, what your corporate title demands of you, and what society tells you to care about. As we grow up, we get so busy managing these outside pressures that the fog thickens. Slowly, the diamond disappears from view, and we lose the ability to speak directly to our own values.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Your value is like a diamond, but now covered behind think fogsBut YOU ARE UNIQUE — not because of your titles, but because of who you really are. And once you peel back that floating apple and reconnect with the raw, unfiltered version of yourself, you can start writing a story that is genuinely yours.
To help you step out from behind the armor of your external identity, I’ve put together a list of six challenging, deeply honest questions. They aren’t meant to look good on a resume; they are meant to make you look inward.

• Question 1: If your life were driven by a single hunger, what would it be?

• Question 2: What is a single scene from your childhood that still burns with warmth?

• Question 3: Which legend about yourself is technically true, but emotionally false?

• Question 4: If someone who dislikes you were to write about you, what would they get exactly right?

• Question 5: If someone who loves you were to write about you, what would they refuse to admit?

• Question 6: What is the gift that became your curse?

From Curated Profile to the Brutally Honest Truth
Sitting down to answer these by yourself can feel daunting. It’s not just that looking into a mirror that deeply takes courage — it’s that a blank page lacks the context we need to actually start thinking. Without a sounding board to challenge us, our minds default to the same old scripted answers.
To make it a little easier, a friend of mine built an open-source tool on GitHub that turns Claude into an intuitive autobiography interviewer. It holds the space for you — asking these kinds of heavy questions, listening to your raw, unfiltered thoughts, and gently weaving them into a narrative.
But what makes this truly beautiful isn’t just using it on yourself. You can take these exact questions and use them to unlock the stories of your parents or grandparents. The most profound, soul-stirring stories don’t always belong to the people who are the most articulate or polished at self-expression. Often, the most fascinating, deeply interesting stories who lived incredibly adventurous, gritty lives shaped by quiet, wise choices.
Historically, capturing those moments required you to be a master interviewer. AI breaks that barrier down completely. It acts as your co-host, helping you ask the right, gentle follow-up questions and capturing their raw spoken memories before they are lost to time. It’s a way to hand them a microphone and say, I see you.
To test it out, I wanted to push the tool to its limits. I copy-pasted my LinkedIn profile along with my Medium link directly into the prompt to give the AI context on how I think and write. It ended up generating six deeply targeted, intensely personal questions. I sat with them for an entire hour, one by one, typing out my brutally honest answers.
When the AI generated the final narrative, seeing how deeply it understood the parts of me I usually hide hit me hard — and the tears just started coming. It was a strange, beautiful feeling to be fully “seen” by a machine when I had spent so long hiding from myself.
The final story it produced is a reflection of my truest self. To be honest, I feel a little scared and hesitant putting it out there. It feels incredibly exposed. But if we want to live authentically, we have to start somewhere. I am finally choosing to lower the apple and publish it below. And perhaps, by dropping my own armor, it might give someone else on the other side of the world the courage to do the same.

The Surface
The warmest place in my childhood was a table I had to take a bus to reach.
My mother worked late, so after school I rode across Macau to my grandmother’s. My younger cousin was there, six years behind me, and the home helper, and dinner. I don’t remember what we ate. I remember laughing at nothing — the kind of laughing that has no reason and needs none. That was the one room where nothing was expected of me. I could just be there. I have spent the rest of my life discovering how rare that room was, because I never found another like it, and eventually I stopped looking and started building surfaces instead.
I got very good at surfaces.
If you met me at work you would call me easy. Likable. The person who makes the deal go smoothly. But beneath that pleasant exterior was a relentless, quiet hunger. The world had a verdict ready for a girl from a small city, a mediocre student, the child of a single parent, a working mom. My response wasn’t to argue; it was to keep moving toward the absolute edge. Every time I faced a crossroads, I simply chose the frontier — the harder market, the next border, the high-stakes rooms. It wasn’t about hitting a specific goal or proving a point; it was just that whenever a choice appeared, my instinct was always to move.
That hunger carried me out of Macau to the hospitality school in Las Vegas, through the business arenas of Barcelona, into the hyper-growth engines of Amazon and Alibaba, and finally to Tencent. I became the person companies sent to the edge because I could manage the turbulence.
I spent my first thirty-five years being too pleasant to fight with. The cost did not appear on any review. The cost was that for those thirty-five years I lost track of the person underneath the pleasantness. She was down there the whole time — the one with opinions, the one who, behind the warm face, quietly despised people and never let it reach her mouth. I ran a private courtroom no one could see. I smiled and passed sentence in the same breath. It was only through coaching, recently, that I learned to watch my own thoughts from a step back, like a third person, and I did not entirely like what I found. I am not as accepting as I have always let the world believe.
My husband uses a shorter word. He says I am selfish. My reflex is not to deny it but to interrogate it — what does selfish even mean? — which is, I now see, just the courtroom turning on itself, arguing the definition so I don’t have to accept the charge. I have wondered whether even my new goodness is another kind of taking: whether I coach and write and build AI learning tools with my daughter to extract a better version of myself from the world. I don’t know. I only know I feel something real when I support someone, and I can’t square that feeling with suspicion. I have left it unsquared. Some things should stay that way.
For years, I haven’t had any job promotions, and no one in those glass towers ever told me why. I filled that silence with the oldest story I had: it’s just blind luck. I told myself it was a just right timing with the right skills that allowed a girl like me to slip into these Fortune 500 tech giants. Because no one ever looked me in the eye to say I was good enough for the next level, I assumed I didn’t really belong at this altitude.
It was only recently, through relentless self-reflection, I finally looked back at the weight of my own life experience. Slowly, a new realization took hold: I began to wonder if it was more than just luck. I started to believe that — perhaps the luck was actually a form of skill all along.
Now, late and entirely against my own nature, I am trying to change. Over these past few years, I have started deliberately cultivating my capacity to do the hardest things: to say no, and to tell the raw truth. But the transition is a brutal internal war. It eats up my energy. My immediate reflex is still to shrink back, to avoid the discomfort entirely. When a conflict inevitably happens, I spend hours afterward rehearsing what I should have done differently. Yet, when the next conflict arrives, the old programming takes over. I let it slide again. Then the guilt sets in, and I blame myself for still hiding — even from my family, where most of the genuine Helen remains buried underground.
And this is the part I cannot yet fix. At my own dinner table, with my own children, my mind runs ahead — worrying, planning, elsewhere. I am trying to learn to simply be there. It does not come easily.
I took a bus across a city, once, toward a table where I could just laugh at nothing. I built the table this time. I am still learning how to arrive.

How to Reclaim Your Story
If you want to step out from behind your own curated screen — or capture the unwritten history of someone you love — here is how to hand the microphone to the machine.
1. Install the Tool
Depending on the platform you use, open your terminal and run the appropriate setup command:
For Claude Users:
Bash
mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills
git clone https://github.com/chengjialu8888/life-autobiography-writer.git ~/.claude/skills/life-autobiography-writerFor Codex Users:
Bash
mkdir -p ~/.codex/skills
git clone https://github.com/chengjialu8888/life-autobiography-writer.git ~/.codex/skills/life-autobiography-writer2. Pick Your Entry Point
Once installed, initiate your session by pasting one of these two prompts directly into the chat:

• Option 1: The Blank Slate for Deep Examination
“Use $life-autobiography-writer to interview me. First, ask me 10 sharp, deep questions — including [Paste the six questions listed above] — one by one. Do not write the narrative yet; let’s have the conversation first.”

• Option 2: The Digital Mirror for Hyper-Personalized Questions
“Use $life-autobiography-writer to parse my LinkedIn, Instagram, and personal profile: [Paste your text or links here]. Build a provisional life map for me. Then, ask me targeted follow-up questions like these [Paste the six questions listed above] before you begin drafting.”

We spend our entire lives assembling armor — building profiles, refining metrics, and engineering the perfect distance between who we are and what the world sees. But the architecture of a real life isn’t found in a flawless summary. It liv
